1: ʾāmar nābāl bĕlibbô ʾên ʾĕlōhîm (ʾāmar nāḇāl bĕlibbô ʾên ʾĕlōhîm): 'The fool says in his heart, 'There is no God.'' The noun nābāl ('fool') in Hebrew does not primarily denote intellectual deficiency but moral and relational failure — the nābāl is one who is morally obtuse, living without reference to God or covenant. The phrase bĕlibbô ('in his heart') again signals practical atheism: the claim is not philosophical but operational. The result: hišḥîtû hiṭʿîbû ʿălîlâ, 'they are corrupt, they do abominable works' — the inner denial produces outward moral collapse. Paul quotes Psalms 14:1-3 (with additions from Pss 5 and 140) in Romans 3:10-12 to establish universal sinfulness.
2: YHWH miššāmayim hišqîp ʿal-bĕnê ʾādām (YHWH miššāmayim hišqîp ʿal-bĕnê ʾāḏām): 'The LORD looks down from heaven on the children of man.' The hiphil hišqîp ('to look down, peer over') places YHWH at elevated vantage — an anthropomorphism for omniscience. The purpose: lirʾôt hăyēš maśkîl dōrēš ʾet-ʾĕlōhîm, 'to see if there are any who understand, who seek after God.' The participle maśkîl (from śākal, 'to have insight/wisdom') describes someone who grasps reality rightly — the opposite of the nābāl. The divine investigation sets up the devastating verdict of Psalms 14:3.
3: hakkōl sār yaḥdāw neʾĕlāḥû (hakkōl sār yaḥḏāw neʾĕlāḥû): 'They have all turned aside; together they have become corrupt.' The all-inclusive hakkōl ('all') and yaḥdāw ('together') close every exit: there is no exception. The niphal neʾĕlāḥû ('become sour/corrupt') uses a term related to fermented milk that has turned — the image is of thoroughgoing contamination. Paul's citation in Romans 3:12-18 extends this to add quotations from Psalms 5, 140, and Isaiah 59, building a catena of human sinfulness — all humanity, Jew and Gentile, stands condemned before the bar of the law.
4: hălōʾ yādĕʿû kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen (hălōʾ yāḏĕʿû kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen): 'Have they no knowledge, all the evildoers?' The rhetorical question expresses shocked incomprehension. The phrase ʾōkĕlê ʿammî ʾāklû leḥem, 'they eat up my people as they eat bread,' uses the image of consuming bread — casual, daily, without moral awareness — to describe the exploitation of the ʿam ('people') by the pōʿălê ʾāwen ('workers of iniquity'). The additional indictment wĕʾet-YHWH lōʾ qārāʾû, 'and they do not call upon the LORD,' is the cultic symptom of the practical atheism declared in Psalms 14:1.
5: šām pāḥădû pāḥad (šām pāḥăḏû pāḥaḏ): 'There they were in great terror.' The cognate accusative pāḥădû pāḥad ('they feared fear') is intensive — overwhelming dread. The phrase kî ʾĕlōhîm bĕdôr ṣaddîq, 'for God is with the generation of the righteous,' reveals the source of the wicked's terror: they discover that the God they denied (Psalms 14:1) is actually present, defending the righteous (ṣaddîq) they were exploiting. The reversal echoes the Psalter's pervasive theme: the practical atheist who lives as though God does not see is surprised by the reality of divine presence.
6: ʿăṣat ʿānî tābîšû (ʿăṣaṯ ʿānî ṯāḇîšû): 'You would shame the plans of the poor.' The verb bôš ('shame, humiliate') in the hiphil tābîšû describes the wicked's attempt to undermine the counsel of the ʿānî ('poor, afflicted'). The response: kî YHWH maḥsēhû, 'because the LORD is his refuge.' The word maḥseh ('refuge, shelter') from ḥāsâ ('take refuge') — the same root as Psalms 11:1 — positions YHWH as the answer to the wicked's attack on the poor. What the wicked do not reckon with is that their victim has a protector they cannot see.
7: mî yittēn miṣṣiyyôn yĕšûʿat yiśrāʾēl (mî yittēn miṣṣiyyôn yĕšûʿaṯ yiśrāʾēl): 'Oh that salvation for Israel would come out of Zion!' The exclamatory mî yittēn ('who will give? / oh that!') shifts from individual crisis to national hope. The phrase bĕšûb YHWH šĕbût ʿammô, 'when the LORD restores the fortunes of his people,' uses šûb šĕbût — an idiom for comprehensive restoration (used also in Jeremiah 30:18; Job 42:10). This verse transforms the psalm from individual lament about fools to communal hope: the deliverance from practical atheism at every level — personal and national — issues from Zion, the place of divine kingship.
